The engineers were doing their jobs correctly; the system failed because the system of which they were a module had been compromised upstream.
themes.implications: The Widder & Nafus finding should change every AI governance training program: it's not enough to train developers to care more about AI safety. You need to change the incentive structures, information flows, and organizational boundaries that currently make AI supply chain responsibility feel like someone else's job.

themes.name: The AI Audit Gap: Why Current AI Audit Regimes Would Not Have Caught LiteLLM
themes.description: Birhane et al. (p140) taxonomize AI audit practices and find they focus primarily on model behavior (fairness, accuracy, safety outputs). The LiteLLM compromise was a software supply chain attack on the build infrastructure — invisible to any audit that evaluates model outputs, training data, or API behavior. This reveals a fundamental gap in the AI audit ecosystem: it has developed around the AI model as the unit of analysis, but the actual attack surface of AI systems is the full software stack from dependencies to deployment infrastructure. Raji et al. (p142) explain why this gap persists: auditors face structural pressures to assess what's measurable and reportable, not what's consequential. Model fairness metrics are concrete and publishable; build pipeline security is abstract and operationally sensitive. The AEF-1 standard (p143) addresses evaluator access conditions, but no current AI audit standard requires examination of the supply chain that produces the AI system. This gap means that financial institutions relying on AI audits for assurance may have false confidence: they have audited the model, not the system. The structural fix requires integrating software supply chain security standards (like SLSA, SPDX AI BOM) into AI audit scope.
themes.implications: Financial institutions should not accept AI audit reports as sufficient assurance for AI supply chain security. AI audit scope must include software supply chain practices: dependency management, build pipeline security, CI/CD configuration, and sub-processor visibility. This is a gap that existing AI governance frameworks do not address.

themes.name: From Point-in-Time Certification to Continuous Trust: Why AI Trust Properties Degrade and What That Means for Governance
themes.description: Grogan's AgentFacts (p146) introduces the critical concept of dynamic metadata with time-to-live freshness — the recognition that AI agent capabilities, permissions, and compliance status change over time. This challenges the prevailing model of AI trust: point-in-time certification (annual audit, upfront due diligence, one-time vendor assessment). The LiteLLM incident illustrates the structural problem with point-in-time approaches: a library that was clean at procurement could be compromised between one update cycle and the next. The AEF-1 standard (p143) implicitly acknowledges this by requiring ongoing access conditions, not just initial assessment. Homewood et al. (p144) introduce the question of when compliance reviews occur: pre-deployment (point-in-time), periodic (low-frequency), or continuous (high-frequency). Continuous approaches are most appropriate for dynamic AI systems but are also most resource-intensive. The governance question this raises: what is the right certification-refresh frequency for AI systems that can change behavior with every model update, fine-tuning, or dependency change? The financial services answer likely varies by risk tier: high-stakes AI systems require continuous or near-continuous assurance; lower-stakes systems can accept periodic review. But the sector currently lacks the infrastructure to deliver continuous assurance for most AI systems.
themes.implications: The point-in-time certification model is inadequate for AI systems that degrade or change continuously. Financial institutions need to develop risk-tiered refresh requirements for AI trust properties: some things should be certified once (fundamental model architecture), some should be monitored continuously (API behavior in production), and some should be assessed on change (fine-tune or update events).

themes.name: MAS TPRM vs APRA CPS 230/234: Neither Framework Was Built for AI Software Supply Chain Risk
themes.description: The MAS Consultation Paper on TPRM (March 2026) extends existing outsourcing guidelines to cover all third-party arrangements, including AI. However, the framework is grounded in traditional service outsourcing logic: risk assessment, due diligence, contracting, monitoring, and termination. The AI BOM approach (p145) and AgentFacts KYA standard (p146) reveal that these frameworks have a fundamental blind spot: they focus on the vendor relationship (is the vendor reliable?) rather than the product provenance (what's actually inside the AI system the vendor is providing?). The LiteLLM case would not be addressed by MAS TPRM requirements: a financial institution could have conducted perfect due diligence on LiteLLM the company, signed ironclad contracts with LiteLLM, and still been compromised by a malicious PyPI package that LiteLLM unknowingly used. APRA CPS 230 and CPS 234 similarly focus on operational risk and security controls at the organizational level, not on the software supply chain that produces AI systems. Neither framework requires AI BOMs, software provenance documentation, or continuous monitoring of AI system components. The gap: financial regulators are applying industrial-age third-party risk frameworks to digital-age AI supply chains, and the mismatch creates exploitable vulnerability.
themes.implications: Regulators and consultants need to develop AI-specific third-party risk management frameworks that incorporate software provenance, AI BOM requirements, and continuous sub-processor monitoring. Existing TPRM frameworks provide necessary but not sufficient coverage.

themes.name: Verification Collapse: Synthetic Reality Attacks the Conditions of Trust, Not Just the Content of Claims
themes.description: Trust in AI degrades not only when outputs are wrong, but when the surrounding verification environment is polluted by synthetic content, synthetic identity, and synthetic interaction. In that environment, provenance becomes harder to establish, institutional verification routines become costlier, and trust claims lose checkability even when individual artifacts look plausible.
themes.implications: AI trust programs should treat provenance, identity, and verification workflow as core control infrastructure. A model cannot be called trustworthy if the institution cannot still verify what inputs, actors, and interactions are real.

themes.name: Oversight Must Follow Verifyability, Not Workflow Position
themes.description: The strongest cross-source pattern in this session is that human oversight only works where humans can still add independent evaluative agency. Better traces, more interface detail, or nominal review steps do not guarantee better judgment. Meaningful oversight depends on solve–verify asymmetry, role-specific harm models, and concentration of human attention at points where review is tractable and decision-changing.
themes.implications: Governance should stop specifying oversight in spatial terms (“in the loop”) and instead specify where humans can still verify, challenge, reroute, or stop outcomes with realistic authority and evidence.

themes.name: From Traceability to Compensation Readiness: Borderless AI Harms Outgrow Local Fault Models
themes.description: As AI harms become transboundary and supply-chain embedded, liability regimes built around local fault attribution and individual human review become less adequate. The governance burden shifts toward foreseeable design responsibility, pooled or channelled compensation mechanisms, and institutional readiness to remediate harms that cannot be cleanly traced to one reviewer or one jurisdiction.
themes.implications: Liability for agentic AI should attach more to design, deployment, monitoring, and compensation architecture than to the fiction that each harmful act could have been individually reviewed by a human.

themes.name: Trust Property Decomposition: Temporal Degradation Profiles Are First-Principles, Not Framework Detail
themes.description: The supervisor's provenance question has a first-principles answer: trust properties decompose into categories with fundamentally different temporal dynamics. (1) Design-Time-Certifiable properties: interpretability architecture, fairness constraints built into model structure, privacy mechanisms (differential privacy), formal verification of safety properties. These are properties of the model's structure, not its behavior, and can be assessed before deployment. (2) Continuously-Monitored properties: calibration drift, alignment stability, robustness under distribution shift, emergent behaviors in new contexts. These are behavioral properties that degrade over time and require post-deployment process monitoring. (3) Change-Triggered properties: performance on new task types, robustness to adversarial adaptation, competence on new data distributions. These can only be assessed at the moment of change (new deployment context, model update, fine-tuning event). The key implication from Ferrario (p093) and Lukas (p095): in Shifting Regime domains (finance, medicine, policy), even the 'certifiable once' properties require post-deployment monitoring because the deployment environment itself changes the effective properties of the system. The Fidelity Paradox shows that training accuracy is not just an unreliable proxy for robustness — it is inversely correlated with robustness in non-stationary domains.
themes.implications: Stop using 'trust framework maturity' as the primary lens. Instead: decompose each trust claim into its temporal properties and apply the appropriate governance mechanism — certification, continuous monitoring, or change-triggered assessment.

themes.name: Reliance Is a Risk-Management Proxy, Not Trust: Why This Distinction Changes Everything
themes.description: The philosophical literature (McLeod, Durán & Pozzi p101) makes a sharp distinction: trust is an attitude; trustworthiness is a property; reliance is the behavioral outcome. In AI contexts, reliance is what happens when epistemic access is too costly or unavailable — you use proxies (model cards, SOC 2 audits, benchmark scores, regulatory certifications) to manage the risk of relying on the AI. These proxies are not the same as epistemic access to the AI's trustworthiness. Quattrociocchi (p094) shows why this gap is structural: LLMs produce epistemically ungrounded but linguistically fluent outputs (Epistemia), so surface-level evaluation (benchmark scores, human preference ratings) measures fluency, not reliability. Lukas (p095) shows that even benchmark-proven models can be anti-robust in Shifting Regime domains. The practical implication: organisations that conflate reliance on AI with actual trustworthiness are managing the appearance of trust, not trust itself. The SOC 2 audit is evidence of process reliability, not outcome reliability. The model card is evidence of disclosed properties, not verified alignment. Neither closes the epistemic gap.
themes.implications: Trust programs should be explicit about what they are: risk-management programs for reliance on AI, not epistemic programs for establishing genuine trustworthiness. This changes what you audit, what you monitor, and what you disclose.

themes.name: Meaningful Human Oversight = Operational Skill, Not Documentation: The Aviation Inflection Point
themes.description: The core answer to 'what does meaningful HITL mean at scale' has a first-principles component and a practical component. First-principles: human oversight only works where humans can add independent evaluative agency — the ability to understand, judge, verify, redirect, or substitute the AI's actions (from p148's operative/evaluative agency distinction, confirmed by the Strata p102 HITL analysis). If the human cannot verify the AI's reasoning, they cannot meaningfully oversee it. Second-principles: the aviation parallel. After the 1970s-80s accident wave, airlines did not just add more pilots — they redesigned oversight as an operational skill through CRM: trained judgment, simulator practice, structured procedures, and no-blame debriefs. Enterprise AI is at the same inflection point. organisations that implement HITL as documentation (checklists, approval workflows, oversight diagrams) without training humans to exercise real judgment under realistic pressure have governance theater, not governance. The automation complacency finding (the more reliable the AI appears, the less vigilant the human overseer) means that passive monitoring degrades precisely when it's most needed.
themes.implications: HITL programs need: (1) risk-tiered oversight modes (HITL/HOTL/HOT by decision type), (2) simulation-based training for approvers, (3) time-boxed decision lanes, (4) active mechanisms to combat automation complacency. Documentation-only programs will fail under scrutiny.

themes.name: Agentic AI Liability: Diffused Accountability Creates Structural Gaps, and Courts Are Starting to Close Them
themes.description: The Global Legal Insights analysis (p099) identifies the core problem: in agentic AI supply chains, every actor — developers, data curators, compliance teams, executives — has plausible deniability, producing 'responsibility gaps' where victims cannot identify a defendant. The Thomson Geer analysis (p098) shows that Australian law applies existing doctrines directly, but the non-deterministic behavior of agentic AI complicates the foreseeability and control elements of negligence. The Harvard JOLT analysis (p096) shows courts are moving toward a 'reasonable AI system' standard — evaluating whether the organisation had adequate governance, oversight architecture, and monitoring — rather than asking whether any individual AI action was adequately reviewed. This is a structural shift: liability is migrating from individual action review to systemic governance adequacy. Three concrete solutions are emerging: (1) channeling liability to a single entity (deployer/operator) with mandatory insurance (nuclear liability model), (2) mandatory pre-market certification for high-risk AI, (3) layered responsibility frameworks that proportionally allocate liability across the supply chain. The Air Canada chatbot precedent (p098) shows organisations cannot disclaim responsibility for AI representations.
themes.implications: Financial institutions deploying agentic AI need: (1) explicit authority mapping for AI agents, (2) identity governance that technically enforces approval checkpoints, (3) stress-tested liability scenarios, (4) monitoring systems that create court-defensible evidence of governance adequacy.

themes.name: Singapore MAS Thematic Review Model: Regulatory Empiricism Beats Framework Theorizing
themes.description: Singapore MAS (p100) represents the most concrete success case of regulator-led AI governance improvement. Rather than waiting for a perfect framework, MAS used its existing supervisory powers to conduct a thematic review of banks' actual AI model risk management practices, documented what good practice looked like across institutions, and published it as industry guidance. This is regulatory empiricism: building governance guidance from observed field practice rather than top-down theoretical framework design. The alternative — prescriptive frameworks designed in advance of field observation — consistently produces governance requirements that are either too generic to be useful or too specific to match actual deployment contexts. The MAS model has three distinctive features: (1) it starts from observed practice, not theoretical design; (2) it publishes findings so the industry can self-assess; (3) it updates as practice evolves. For APRA/ASIC: a thematic review of actual AI model risk management practices across major institutions, with published findings and guidance, would be more valuable than new guidance documents that attempt to specify requirements in advance of field evidence.
themes.implications: The most valuable thing a regulator can do for AI governance is observe what good practice actually looks like in the field and publish it. APRA and ASIC should conduct thematic reviews now, even if the frameworks are incomplete.
